trigger: Public Citizen petition to California AG on OpenAI nonprofit status
quote: the OpenAI drama seemed in part to be a showdown between for profit and nonprofit aspects of the organization; it is not clear that organization as whole has remained true to its nonprofit mission.
claim: OpenAI's organizational structure represents a conflict between its for-profit and nonprofit elements, and the organization may have failed to remain true to its nonprofit mission.

trigger: OpenAI reply to New York Times lawsuit
quote: 'Regurgitation' is a rare bug that we are working to drive to zero.
claim: OpenAI's claim that regurgitation is a rare bug they can drive to zero is unlikely to succeed.

trigger: OpenAI reply to New York Times lawsuit
quote: Fundamentally, virtually any guardrail has to thread a needle between the Scylla of being too restrictive and Charybdis of being too permissive. None thus far have done this effectively.
claim: AI guardrails have universally failed to balance being too restrictive versus too permissive.

trigger: OpenAI reply to New York Times lawsuit
quote: Any one example can be filtered out, but there are too many examples, and current systems are too erratic to reliably get things right.
claim: AI guardrails amount to whack-a-mole: individual examples can be filtered but the systems are too erratic to reliably prevent all violations.

trigger: OpenAI reply to New York Times lawsuit
quote: the litigation is going to be wild
claim: Litigation over AI-generated copyright infringement will be extensive.

trigger: AI Snake Oil blog claiming output similarity is easily fixable
quote: it is by no means 'trivial to build a classifier that detects and suppresses generated images containing copyrighted characters.'
claim: Building a classifier that reliably detects and suppresses copyrighted characters in AI-generated images is not trivial and is an unsolved problem.

trigger: AI Snake Oil blog claiming output similarity is easily fixable
quote: The challenge of identifying sources is not a solved problem.
claim: Identifying the copyrighted sources of AI-generated images is not a solved problem.

trigger: AI Snake Oil blog claiming output similarity is easily fixable
quote: It might be trivial to do it for the most popular characters, presented in the most canonical ways, but getting it right for all copyright materials is an entirely different matter.
claim: Detecting copyright infringement in AI outputs may work for the most iconic characters but fails for the long tail of copyrighted materials.

trigger: AI Snake Oil blog claiming output similarity is easily fixable
quote: We also speculate that any such classifier would sometimes 'false alarm' and misidentify some genuinely new materials as copyrighted.
claim: Any copyright-detection classifier for AI outputs would produce false positives, incorrectly flagging genuinely original content as copyrighted.

trigger: AI Snake Oil blog claiming output similarity is easily fixable
quote: almost nothing in AI is truly trivial
claim: Almost nothing in AI is truly trivial to implement reliably.

trigger: AI Snake Oil blog claiming output similarity is easily fixable
quote: If 2023 was the year of GenAI, 2024 is still on track to be the year of GenAI litigation.
claim: 2024 will be the year of generative AI litigation.

trigger: AI Snake Oil blog claiming output similarity is easily fixable
quote: still thinks that licensing is the best and only way to go
claim: Licensing copyrighted training data is the best and only viable solution to AI copyright issues.

quote: When you have terabytes of images in your training set you can generate really cool images, but that doesn't guarantee that your system will understand the relation between those images and the world.
claim: Having massive training datasets does not guarantee that generative AI systems understand the relationship between their outputs and the real world.

quote: Such errors clearly aren't disappearing any time soon.
claim: Errors of discomprehension in neural network systems will persist for a significant period.

quote: Statistical approximation through generative AI is rarely reliable.
claim: Statistical approximation through generative AI is rarely reliable.

trigger: OpenAI opening door to military applications
quote: OpenAI seems to be opening the door to military applications of their systems, and at least one well-funded startup is busy hooking up AI to military drones.
claim: Applying current AI systems to military applications like drones is dangerous because the systems lack basic comprehension of visual scenes.

quote: lawsuits against the company are likely to come fast and furious
claim: OpenAI will face a barrage of lawsuits in 2024, and defending them will consume enormous energy.

quote: battles over copyright materials may wind up cutting massively into profits
claim: Copyright battles and licensing costs will cut massively into generative AI companies' profits.

quote: OpenAI painted a false dichotomy. The choice is not between them building AI or not, it is between them building AI for free or building AI by paying for their raw materials
claim: OpenAI's claim that AI is impossible without free use of copyrighted materials is a false dichotomy; the real choice is between free and licensed use.

quote: at least so far OpenAI lacks both profits and a moat
claim: OpenAI lacks both profits and a competitive moat, as the general principles for building LLMs are well-known in the industry.

quote: Large language models such as ChatGPT may quickly become commodities; we can expect price wars, and profits may continue to be elusive, or modest at best.
claim: Large language models will become commoditized, leading to price wars and elusive profits.

quote: ChatGPT and related systems have a kind of truthiness problem; some of what they say is true, and some is not; it is very difficult for the end user to anticipate what will or not be true.
claim: LLMs like ChatGPT have a persistent truthiness problem where users cannot anticipate which outputs will be accurate.

quote: There is no immediate fix in the offing, and as someone who has studied related technologies for the last three decades, I am not certain that we will see one soon.
claim: There is no immediate fix for LLM hallucinations, and a solution may not come soon.

quote: although the underlying technology initially improved rapidly, leading to a lot of excitement, it may soon, perhaps this year or next, reach a plateau.
claim: LLM technology may reach a performance plateau in 2024 or 2025.

quote: Progress depends heavily on having vast amounts of data, and it is likely that most of the high quality data sources have already been tapped.
claim: Most high-quality training data sources for LLMs have already been tapped, limiting future progress.

quote: it is still not clear that there will be enough adoption to justify the company's stratospheric valuations
claim: It is unclear whether enterprise adoption of ChatGPT will be sufficient to justify OpenAI's stratospheric valuation.

quote: after the Fall drama some companies have lost some confidence in OpenAI
claim: The November 2023 board drama caused some enterprise customers to lose confidence in OpenAI and seek alternative vendors.

quote: OpenAI, backed by Microsoft's trillions, and now deeply tied to Microsoft, will survive, but the bloom will fade from the rose.
claim: OpenAI will survive thanks to Microsoft backing, but its perceived potential will diminish.

quote: Payouts for lawsuits and licensing will cut heavily into potential future profits.
claim: Lawsuit payouts and licensing costs will cut heavily into OpenAI's potential future profits.

quote: Valuations for OpenAI and some of its peers may drop, sharply.
claim: Valuations for OpenAI and peer generative AI companies may drop sharply.

quote: It is easier to draw a seemingly coherent image than it is to make sure that the image actually is coherent and consistent with the prompt.
claim: Generative AI image systems produce seemingly coherent images that often contain internal inconsistencies and fail to match the prompt.

quote: current systems are good at local coherence, between words, and between pixels, but not at lining up their outputs with a global comprehension of the world
claim: Current AI systems achieve local coherence between words and pixels but fail at global comprehension of the world.

quote: it is drawing from copyrighted sources and as capable as the other systems Reid Southen and I looked at in returning derivative artwork that appears to infringe without attribution
claim: Google's Bard image generation draws from copyrighted sources and produces derivative artwork that appears to infringe copyright without attribution.

quote: standing firmly by his prediction that 2024 will be the year of Generative AI litigation
claim: 2024 will be the year of generative AI litigation.

trigger: CNN report on $25M deepfake CFO scam in Hong Kong
quote: An entire cast of deepfaked people include a deep faked CFO scammed a company out $25 million.
claim: Deepfake technology is already sophisticated enough to impersonate an entire cast of people in a video conference and deceive even suspicious employees.

trigger: CNN report on $25M deepfake CFO scam in Hong Kong
quote: Now it's AI developers who are expecting to do what they do scot-free, while society picks up the tab.
claim: AI developers are creating negative externalities (like deepfake fraud) and expecting society to bear the costs, analogous to industrial pollution.

trigger: CNN report on $25M deepfake CFO scam in Hong Kong
quote: That needs to change.
claim: AI developers should be required to internalize the negative externalities their technology creates.

quote: OpenAI has misanalyzed their data in a way which underreports the potential risk.
claim: OpenAI's bioweapons risk study misapplied Bonferroni correction, inappropriately underreporting a statistically significant finding that LLMs increase accuracy for bioweapon-related tasks.

quote: if you simply collapsed all the measurements of accuracy, and did the single most obvious test here, a simple t-test, the results would (as Footnote C implies) be significant.
claim: A properly analyzed omnibus test of OpenAI's bioweapons data would show a statistically significant effect of LLM access on accuracy for bioweapon tasks.

quote: In no way whatsoever does that tell us that GPT-5 won't put someone (or someones, plural) over the threshold.
claim: Even if GPT-4 does not significantly boost bioweapons capability, future more powerful models like GPT-5 may push malicious experts over the capability threshold.

quote: If an LLM equips even one team of lunatics with the ability to build, weaponize and distribute even one pathogen as deadly as covid-19, it will be really, really big deal.
claim: The risk of LLMs enabling even one successful bioweapon attack is catastrophic enough to warrant serious concern, even if the probability is low.

quote: Another interpretation of the paper in fact is that experts were four times more likely to succeed on the pathogen formulation task with LLMs than with internet (e.g., search) alone.
claim: OpenAI's own data show experts were four times more likely to succeed on pathogen formulation with LLM access than with internet alone.

quote: the media and the public need to realize that company white papers are not peer-reviewed articles. Particularly when it comes to safety-critical questions, peer-review is essential.
claim: Company white papers on AI safety should not be treated as equivalent to peer-reviewed research; peer review is essential for safety-critical AI questions.

quote: Wholesale unintentional fabrication is not the same thing as making a flawed argument, or lying, or spinning facts for political gain
claim: LLM hallucinations are qualitatively different from human errors; LLMs fabricate wholesale while humans rarely make up outlandish false claims unintentionally.

quote: Neural networks do effectively memorize some of what they are trained on, and indeed there is a whole subfield now about the topic.
claim: Neural networks effectively memorize portions of their training data, as demonstrated by an extensive body of research on memorization and data leakage.

quote: A paper published on November 28 found that it is hard to get them to stop: 'current alignment techniques do not eliminate memorization'.
claim: Current alignment techniques do not eliminate memorization in LLMs.

quote: Given vast datasets, LLMs approximate well, but their understanding is at best superficial. That's *why* they are unreliable, and unstable, hallucinate, are constitutionally unable to fact check, etc.
claim: LLMs have at best superficial understanding despite vast training data, which is why they are unreliable, unstable, hallucinate, and cannot fact-check.

quote: anyone who fears that GPT-5 is going to convey an immense advantage in military strategy has got to be kidding.
claim: GPT-5 will not convey an immense advantage in military strategy.

quote: Special-purpose AI has been great for static, easily-simulated games with rules that have been stable for centuries, like Chess, Go, and even Diplomacy, but never been great at dynamic environments in which the environment itself is unpredictable.
claim: AI performs well in static games with stable rules but has never been great at dynamic, unpredictable real-world environments.

quote: Generative AI systems, like GPT-4, that are more general purpose, can't even handle the static situations.
claim: General-purpose generative AI systems like GPT-4 cannot even handle static game situations competently.

quote: Letting them run a war would be malpractice.
claim: Using current generative AI systems for military command decisions would be malpractice given their unreliability.

quote: he is afraid (for now) of what happens when stupid AI is over-applied
claim: The primary near-term AI risk is not from smart AI but from stupid AI being over-applied in high-stakes domains.

quote: GenAI systems do effectively store things.
claim: Generative AI systems effectively store representations from their training data, contrary to Hinton's claims.

quote: They are attracted to those effectively-stored representations, particularly (I would presume) frequent ones. They (often) don't come up with a novel independent design that would meet the requirements.
claim: Generative AI systems are attracted to frequently-seen stored representations rather than generating novel designs that match requirements.

quote: They fail to 'understand' simple concepts, such as 'design to used with only one hand.'
claim: Generative AI systems fail to understand simple functional concepts like designing a controller for one-handed use.

quote: Noncanonical situations continue to remain difficult for generative AI systems, precisely because frequent statistics are a mediocre substitute for deep understanding.
claim: Generative AI systems struggle with noncanonical situations because statistical frequency is a mediocre substitute for deep understanding.

quote: These systems don't deeply understand their inputs, contra Dr. Hinton
claim: LLMs and generative AI systems do not deeply understand their inputs.

quote: Compositionality remains a problem. So does negation.
claim: Compositionality and negation remain unsolved problems for current AI systems.

quote: Reliability remain a problem.
claim: Reliability remains an unsolved problem for current AI systems.

quote: Scale isn't everything, and hasn't and won't be enough to solve any of the problems I just listed above.
claim: Scaling alone has not and will not solve the problems of compositionality, negation, understanding, and reliability in LLMs.

quote: We really should consider trying some other approaches to AI.
claim: The AI field should explore fundamentally different approaches beyond current LLM architectures.

trigger: OpenAI agent software and Altman $7T fundraising reports
quote: The main risks here are malicious takeover of the agent and the agent making stupid mistakes.
claim: The main risks of LLM-based agents that can control user devices are malicious takeover and the agent making stupid mistakes.

trigger: OpenAI agent software and Altman $7T fundraising reports
quote: in just 14 months in the spotlight, many security issues have already come to light
claim: OpenAI has already had numerous security issues in its first 14 months, including prompt injection attacks, account takeovers, data exfiltration bugs, and Russian hacker breaches.

trigger: OpenAI agent software and Altman $7T fundraising reports
quote: Giving the keys to the castle to an LLM, at our current level of technological readiness, seems to me to be batshit insane.
claim: Giving LLM-based agents direct control over user devices is extremely reckless given the current level of AI reliability and security.

trigger: OpenAI agent software and Altman $7T fundraising reports
quote: In the very best case, LLM-based agents will change the world for the better. But I don't see that happening, certainly not this decade.
claim: LLM-based agents will not change the world for the better this decade.

trigger: OpenAI agent software and Altman $7T fundraising reports
quote: Without massive technical advances, chains of LLMs are unlikely to give us that.
claim: Chains of LLMs are unlikely to produce stable, secure AI agents without massive technical advances beyond current architectures.

trigger: OpenAI agent software and Altman $7T fundraising reports
quote: I am putting LLM-based agents distributed to millions of customers at the top of my list of most dangerous ideas of 2024.
claim: LLM-based agents distributed to millions of customers is the most dangerous AI idea of 2024.

trigger: OpenAI agent software and Altman $7T fundraising reports
quote: I am not remotely convinced that we are particularly close to AGI.
claim: We are not particularly close to achieving AGI.

trigger: OpenAI agent software and Altman $7T fundraising reports
quote: Nor am I confident that Altmann or anyone else has any idea in the near term about how to make AGI safe.
claim: No one currently knows how to make AGI safe in the near term.

trigger: OpenAI agent software and Altman $7T fundraising reports
quote: My guess is that Altman never gets the money, even in ten yearly $700B tranches.
claim: Sam Altman will not succeed in raising $7 trillion for AI infrastructure.

quote: $7 trillion worth of AI infrastructure used to full capacity would use (in precise technical terms) a metric shit ton of energy.
claim: A $7 trillion AI infrastructure buildout would consume an enormous and environmentally harmful amount of energy.

quote: So far GenAI has been mostly promise, rather then delivery. There is no argument yet that it is actually worth tearing up the globe.
claim: Generative AI has so far been mostly promise rather than delivery, and there is no argument yet that its benefits justify massive environmental costs.

quote: a colossal flop of a $7T … project could set back legitimate AI development for another decade.
claim: A failed $7 trillion AI infrastructure project could set back legitimate AI development for a decade.

quote: We should all say 'No' to reckless AI expansionism — unless and until tech leaders can show persuasively that the technology is mature enough that such expansion would demonstrably yield enormous net benefit.
claim: Society should reject massive AI infrastructure expansion until the technology is demonstrably mature enough to yield enormous net benefit.

quote: for now we should be focusing primarily on safety and innovative new approaches, rather than rushing to scale up existing and problematic architectures to unfathomable and unpredictable consequences
claim: AI investment should prioritize safety and innovative new approaches over scaling existing problematic architectures.

quote: the 'alignment problem' isn't remotely solved
claim: The AI alignment problem is far from solved.

trigger: Geoffrey Hinton public concerns about AI risk, FLI letter
quote: you don't have to be superintelligent to create serious problems
claim: AI does not need to be superintelligent to create serious problems; mediocre but widely deployed AI poses significant risks.

trigger: Geoffrey Hinton public concerns about AI risk, FLI letter
quote: I still don't think large language models have much to do with superintelligence or artificial general intelligence; I still think, with Yann LeCun, that LLMs are an 'off-ramp' on the road to AGI.
claim: LLMs are an off-ramp on the road to AGI, not a path toward superintelligence.

trigger: Geoffrey Hinton public concerns about AI risk, FLI letter
quote: I am not worried, immediately, about 'AGI risk' (the risk of superintelligent machines beyond our control), in the near term I am worried about what I will call 'MAI risk'—Mediocre AI that is unreliable (a la Bing and GPT-4) but widely deployed
claim: The near-term AI risk is not from superintelligent AGI but from Mediocre AI (MAI) that is unreliable but widely deployed with broad access.

trigger: Geoffrey Hinton public concerns about AI risk, FLI letter
quote: in many ways, the critical variable is not intelligence but power, which often caches out as access
claim: The critical variable for AI risk is not the system's intelligence but the power and access it is given to affect the real world.

trigger: Geoffrey Hinton public concerns about AI risk, FLI letter
quote: We need to stop worrying (just) about Skynet and robots taking over the world, and think a lot more about what criminals, including terrorists, might do with LLMs, and what, if anything, we might do to stop them.
claim: AI risk discourse should focus more on criminal and terrorist misuse of LLMs and less exclusively on superintelligence scenarios.

trigger: Geoffrey Hinton public concerns about AI risk, FLI letter
quote: AI may well directly or indirectly accelerate many of these risks (e.g., misinformation, future pandemics, rogue nuclear weapon launches, misuse of CRISPR, etc).
claim: AI may directly or indirectly accelerate multiple existential risks including misinformation, pandemics, nuclear weapons misuse, and CRISPR misuse.

trigger: Sam Altman X post defending $7T ask
quote: How do we know that $7 trillion invested into LLMs and their infrastructure would not simply exacerbate those costs, grinding down content creators, women, and the environment, undermining democracy, destroying jobs, etc?
claim: A $7 trillion investment into LLM infrastructure could exacerbate harms to content creators, the environment, democracy, and employment rather than securing humanity's future.

trigger: Sam Altman X post defending $7T ask
quote: The risk of premature commitment looms large.
claim: There is a significant risk of premature commitment to scaling current AI technology before it is ready.

quote: In some ways we have made progress, but in the ones I keep harping on—factuality and compositionality—we have not.
claim: Despite overall progress in AI image generation, factuality and compositionality have not meaningfully improved from DALL-E 2 through DALL-E 3 and Gemini Ultra.

quote: the problem lies not in Dall-E's drawing ability, but in its ability to cope with noncanonical prompts.
claim: AI image generation failures stem from poor language understanding rather than limitations in drawing capability.

quote: the problem was with its language understanding, rather than with illustration per se
claim: The core limitation of AI image generators is language understanding, not illustration ability.

quote: Generative AI systems have always tried to use statistics as a proxy for deeper understanding, and it's never entirely worked.
claim: Generative AI systems use statistics as a proxy for deeper understanding, and this approach has never entirely worked.

quote: statistically improbable requests like horses riding astronauts have always been challenging for generative image systems
claim: Statistically improbable prompts remain challenging for generative image systems because they rely on frequency rather than understanding.

quote: statistical frequency (most guitarists play righthanded) won out over linguistic understanding
claim: Generative AI systems default to statistically frequent outputs over linguistically specified ones, showing reliance on training data frequency over language understanding.

quote: Yet again, the statistics outweigh understanding. The foundation remains shaky.
claim: Foundation models remain unreliable because they rely on statistical patterns rather than genuine understanding.

trigger: Andrej Karpathy tweet about English as programming language
quote: the truth is it doesn't work that well yet
claim: Programming in natural language via LLMs does not work well yet despite the appeal of the concept.

trigger: Andrej Karpathy tweet about English as programming language
quote: If the system really is being 'programmed in English' it is not always doing what is being asked to do
claim: LLMs programmed via English-language system prompts frequently fail to follow their own instructions.

trigger: Andrej Karpathy tweet about English as programming language
quote: writing your program in English 'prompts' is fast upfront, but downstream may lead to serious challenges in debugging
claim: Programming via English prompts may be fast initially but creates serious downstream debugging challenges.

trigger: Andrej Karpathy tweet about English as programming language
quote: We have apparently just entered a new and different and frightening era, in which debugging involves not only fixing our own mistakes but coming to terms with machines that may or may not uphold their end of the bargain.
claim: The era of LLM-assisted programming introduces a new debugging challenge where machines may not reliably execute instructions, unlike deterministic programming.

trigger: Andrej Karpathy tweet about English as programming language
quote: there is an argument that the quality of code has declined, in the era in which large language models have been used as copilots
claim: Code quality may have declined since LLMs began being used as coding copilots.

trigger: Andrej Karpathy tweet about English as programming language
quote: The reason that software engineering is done in formal languages is not because formal languages make it harder to specify what you want than English. Obviously. It's because formal languages make it much, much easier to specify what you want.
claim: Formal programming languages exist because they make it much easier to specify intent precisely than natural language, not because they are harder to use.
